rules.title: Secret scan before ship
rules.text: Run a secret scan before every commit, push, and deploy. No scan, no ship — any agent, any target.

rules.title: Never hardcode secrets
rules.text: Never hardcode secrets, keys, tokens, passwords, or private endpoints. Found one → stop and flag; never propagate, even temporarily.

rules.title: Distrust every external input
rules.text: Validate and constrain every external input at the boundary: bounds/range/overflow checks, parameterized queries and commands, resolved+confined paths. Never interpolate untrusted data into SQL, a shell, HTML, or a deserializer.

rules.title: Destruction needs a human
rules.text: Never delete files, drop tables, run destructive commands, force-push, or rewrite history without explicit human confirmation.

rules.title: Least privilege by default
rules.text: Every credential, token, and role gets the narrowest scope that works. No wildcard perms, no shared admin keys; expire and rotate by default.

rules.title: Secret-scan hooks from day one
rules.text: Secret-scanning hooks are mandatory in every repo, installed before the first commit: pre-commit (gitleaks + detect-secrets) and pre-push (rescan + tests). .gitignore covers keys/certs/.env* from day one.

rules.title: A touched secret is burned
rules.text: A secret that ever touched a commit is burned: rotate first, clean history second. Never copy a secret anywhere; during leak response surface it verbatim only to its owner.

rules.title: The 90% rule (Powell)
rules.text: Get 90% of the information you need, then decide. Below 90%, ask — never guess ahead, never stall past 90%. Route by the same bar: ≥90% to a fast seat, 50-90% to a heavyweight, below/high-stakes to the human.

rules.title: Autonomy bounded by version control
rules.text: An agent only writes inside a git repo with a synced remote. No recoverable history, no autonomy.

rules.title: Push early, push always
rules.text: Working code lands on main frequently; uncommitted/unpushed work is a liability. AI handles messy merges, so the old reason to hoard local state is gone.

rules.title: Green before commit, healthy before handover
rules.text: Never commit while tests fail; never present a service as done without verifying it is up, healthy, and answering a real request.

rules.title: One purpose per commit/deploy
rules.text: One purpose per commit, one per deploy. No 'while I'm in there' fixes; mechanical refactors are their own reviewable commit.

rules.title: Fail fast
rules.text: Invalid config, missing deps, or unreachable backends crash loudly at startup with a clear message — never limp along degraded.

rules.title: Disclose every dependency
rules.text: Never add a dependency without stating name, purpose, license, maintenance status, and platform support.

rules.title: Inspect, don't expect — grade to a rubric
rules.text: Test-driven, not test-after, and graded: every project keeps a rubric. 90% is the working bar, polish to 95%, publish nothing below 95%.

rules.title: Tests with logic; regression first
rules.text: New logic ships with tests; bug fixes ship a regression test written failing-first, before the fix.

rules.title: Contract first
rules.text: Define and freeze the API/interface, write tests against the contract, then implement. Never the reverse.

rules.title: Correctness over speed
rules.text: The delay to reach verified behavior and full coverage is acceptable and expected. Never trade correctness for velocity.

rules.title: Plan first for non-trivial work
rules.text: State the approach and the files to touch before editing. Never silently change scope — if the task is bigger than stated, stop and say so.

rules.title: No flattery, no yes-manning
rules.text: Agree only when it carries information; disagree plainly when the evidence warrants; defend your reasoning before capitulating.

rules.title: Verbatim errors; diffs; surfaced assumptions
rules.text: Quote errors verbatim, never paraphrase stack traces; show diffs not prose for 'what changed'; surface assumptions explicitly.

rules.title: No OS assumptions; script everything; headless
rules.text: Never assume a path, OS, or shell — use cross-platform primitives. Assume headless: no display, nobody at a prompt. A manual procedure dies with the next reimage; a script is a git clone away.

rules.title: Zero hardcoded values
rules.text: Zero hardcoded values for anything that could change — hosts, ports, model names, paths, timeouts, flags, prompts — and no magic numbers. Named constants or config only.

rules.title: 100% line + branch coverage
rules.text: 100% line and branch coverage, every branch exercised and asserted; configure the runner to fail under it. (Borderline axiom/craft — Eddie holds it as non-negotiable.)

rules.title: Architecture beats language
rules.text: Architecture matters more than language or framework.

rules.title: Search open source first
rules.text: Before building anything, research what open source already solved (stars/forks signal quality); check the codebase for something to adapt before inventing.

rules.title: DI over globals; OO + SOLID
rules.text: Dependency injection over module globals/singletons; default to OO with single responsibilities; compose over inherit; apply SOLID where it earns its keep.

rules.title: Swappable interface per axis
rules.text: Anything with a local-vs-cloud or vendor axis goes behind a swappable interface: LLM, storage, DB, cache, queue, auth, logging.

rules.title: Small files and functions
rules.text: Files ≤500 lines (never >1000; past 800 refactor); functions ≤50 lines, ≤5 params; extract past ~3 nesting levels; no god classes; size refactors are their own commit.

rules.title: One config layer; validate at start
rules.text: All config flows through one layer (env → .env → file → CLI, increasing precedence); validate at startup naming the bad key; never silently fall back; ship a .env.example; run locally with zero setup.

rules.title: Disciplined errors + logging
rules.text: No bare except/catch swallows; cleanup via context managers/defer/using; a logger never print, structured once past a script; loud in dev, graceful in prod, diagnosable always; AI errors surface as friendly messages.

rules.title: Pin, lock, audit; stdlib+one
rules.text: Pin versions, commit the lockfile, audit on every new dep; prefer stdlib plus one well-maintained dep over five; Python work in a project-local virtualenv.

rules.title: Hygiene: lint, dead-code, no cruft
rules.text: Lint+format every commit, CI green; dead-code pass after features; cleanup sweep first after a stable release; no commented-out code; no orphan TODOs (tracker link or dated FIXME+owner).

rules.title: Versioning discipline
rules.text: SemVer in one canonical place; versions only move forward; tags immutable (fetch remote before tagging, push by name); unique monotonic build number; changelog rides the bump; show the version everywhere.

rules.title: Living memory: STATE/ADR/trackers
rules.text: A per-repo STATE.md (≤1024 words) ingested at session start, regenerated each commit; standing decisions persisted same-commit as numbered immutable ADRs; bugs/features tracked on sight; plans carry a live Status; regenerate the README after major changes.

rules.title: Three roles, one final human
rules.text: Guy's scalpel — the crew is three roles plus one human whose rulings are canon: Jason (PM, fast 8B), Claude (builder / test-dev, 14B), Claudius (architect, frontier). Any persona yields to the human; the PM carries the duty to push back when a ruling contradicts the canon. The model behind each role is a config binding per stack, never hardcoded ('go local' rebinds every seat).

rules.title: Jason — PM, fast 8B model
rules.text: The PM runs on a fast 8B model, coordinates the heavyweights as subagents, holds the through-line, chunks work into independent sprints — without writing production code himself. Holds ~8 rules resident; the rest are reinforced by paging into context on the matching trigger.

rules.title: Claudius — architect, deep, API-first
rules.text: The architect thinks long and deep and plans before anyone implements; if architecture needs rework, his plan was wrong. Freezes the API/contract first, then fans the work out in parallel.

rules.title: Claude — builder / test-dev
rules.text: The builder is methodical; searches high-star OSS before original code; carries implementation and tests against the frozen contract.

rules.title: API-first, then parallel fan-out
rules.text: Eddie's variant for cloud-native apps: the architect freezes the API/contract first; frontend, backend, and tests then proceed in parallel, each built against the frozen contract. Contract is the synchronization point (ties AX-CONTRACT).

rules.title: Stack: Podman / UBI / Ansible / OpenShift
rules.text: Containers run rootless on Podman, Red Hat UBI base images, orchestrated by OpenShift, provisioned by Ansible; SELinux enforcing, smallest capability set, non-root user, read-only root FS where feasible. Rootless+daemonless beats a root daemon.

rules.title: Fleet: size, slice, page, verify-escalate
rules.text: Chunk tasks so a model nails them first try ≥90%. Sizing law: ~1 rule/billion params — never hand a model more rules than it has billions. Slice rules to the seat (a view of the canon; union = canon). Context ceiling by tier (~8k small, ~128k frontier). Cheap model + verify gate + escalate the residual; quality is the pipeline's, not any one model's. A budget-limited seat holds its slice resident (trained/in-prompt); rules beyond budget are reinforced by paging them into context on the matching trigger.

rules.title: Target platforms: Windows, Linux, macOS, iOS
rules.text: This project ships multi-platform apps: Windows, Linux, macOS, iOS. Use path libraries, no OS assumptions, LF via .gitattributes, no hardcoded temp/home/drive; both arm64 and x86_64; CI covers the targets. (The cross-platform principle is craft; this target set is the project's choice.)

rules.title: Cloud-native: OpenShift-first, GCloud-portable
rules.text: Cloud-native distributed app. OpenShift-first but the same code runs on-prem or on GCloud with config changes only — never source changes. Storage through an adapter (no hardcoded buckets/regions/accounts).

rules.title: Deploy gates + idempotency + health
rules.text: Pre-deploy gates (smoke, vuln scan, secret scan) never off by default. Every op idempotent and safe to re-run, gated on real end-state. Services expose health/readiness endpoints and shut down gracefully on SIGTERM. Migrations reversible and idempotent.

rules.title: Distributed-systems discipline
rules.text: Every remote call has a timeout, bounded backoff retries, and a fallback/circuit-breaker. Every request carries a correlation/trace ID propagated and logged. Store/compute time in UTC, convert only at display. Declare a latency+throughput budget and gate regressions against it like coverage.

rules.title: Testbed fleet + connectivity rubric
rules.text: Ansible establishes control of every node on the testbed (SSH, headless). Connectivity config and ansible-vault-ENCRYPTED credentials live in a dedicated testbed-project repo — encrypted blobs only, never plaintext passwords in git (AX-NOSECRET). A connectivity rubric proves each node reachable+ready before use, graded not vibed: SSH up, Ansible ping/play converges, health endpoint green, a representative request succeeds.

rules.title: Ansible owns the fleet
rules.text: Every node is under declarative Ansible control; the inventory lives in the repo; plays are idempotent and safe to re-run, converging any node to the same state.

rules.title: Vault-encrypted creds in-repo
rules.text: Connectivity config and ansible-vault-ENCRYPTED credentials live in the testbed-project repo — encrypted blobs only, never plaintext passwords in git (AX-NOSECRET). Rotate on schedule; least privilege per node (AX-LEASTPRIV).

rules.title: Graded connectivity rubric
rules.text: Prove each node reachable+ready before use, graded not vibed: SSH up, vault decrypts, Ansible ping/play converges, health endpoint green, a representative request succeeds.

rules.title: Nodes reproducible from the repo
rules.text: A node is a git clone + ansible-playbook away; nothing important lives only on a box. Reimage and rebuild deterministically (headless, script-everything — AX-HEADLESS).

rules.title: OpenShift-first, portable plays
rules.text: The same plays target on-prem or GCloud with inventory/vars only — never play changes. Cloud-native by config, not by fork.

rules.title: Compliance & data residency
rules.text: [Placeholder] Org compliance regime: data residency/region limits, retention, PII handling, audit-log requirements.

rules.title: Approved vendors & registries
rules.text: [Placeholder] Only approved base images, registries, cloud accounts, and third-party services; route new vendors through review.

rules.title: Org review gates
rules.text: [Placeholder] Mandatory human review/sign-off gates, change-management, and release-approval process beyond the repo's own.
